emails.subject: Re: LLC formation questions
emails.content:
Hey Matt,

Good questions. Short answer: it depends on your timeline, but there are reasons to form sooner rather than later.

Advantages of forming now:
- You can start writing off startup expenses (research, conferences, subscriptions)
- Establishes a business entity date which matters for some contracts
- Colorado LLC filing is $50, annual report is $10. Very low cost.

Downsides:
- You'll need a registered agent (can be yourself at your home address, but that's public record)
- Minor bookkeeping overhead

On the S-Corp question, don't bother until you're clearing $40-50k in consulting revenue. LLC taxed as sole prop is fine for the startup phase.

One thing to think about: does your current employer have any non-compete or moonlighting clauses? Worth checking before you file anything.

Let me know if you want to dig into this more on our call next week.

Rebecca Moran
Moran Advisors | Austin, TX

emails.subject: Re: LLC formation questions
emails.content:
Matt,

Yes, registered agent services are common. Colorado has several. Northwest Registered Agent runs about $125/year and keeps your home address off public filings. I use them for several clients.

If discretion is a priority, I'd also recommend:
- Use your personal email only (you're already doing this, good)
- Get a separate business bank account when you're ready to transact
- Don't list the LLC on LinkedIn until you're ready to go public

On timing, January is actually ideal. Clean tax year start. And you can retroactively claim 2024 expenses if you form within a reasonable window of when you started incurring them.

Let's map this out on our next call. I have some templates for the operating agreement too.

Rebecca

emails.subject: Pricing tier structure - need your eyes on this
emails.content:
Rebecca,

I finally put together the pricing tier structure for the consulting services. Been staring at this for two weeks and I can't tell if the numbers make sense anymore.

Tier 1 is the Curriculum Audit at $4,500. That covers a 2-day on-site assessment, a written report with recommendations, and a 30-day follow-up call.

Tier 2 is the Leadership Coaching Package at $8,000 per quarter, which includes monthly 90-minute coaching sessions, email support between sessions, and assessment tools and frameworks.

Tier 3 is the Full Engagement at $18,000 and bundles the curriculum audit, the coaching package, an equity audit component, and quarterly check-ins for one year.

Am I pricing myself out of the market? Most of my potential clients would be mid-size districts. I looked at competitors and I'm landing in the middle but I keep second-guessing.

Would love your feedback before I finalize the business plan section.

Matt

emails.subject: Re: Pricing tier structure - need your eyes on this
emails.content:
Matt,

These look solid. A few thoughts:

Tier 1 is priced right for the ed-consulting space. Some people charge more for 2-day on-sites but you're new so competitive pricing makes sense for the first year.

Tier 2 feels slightly low. $8k/quarter for monthly coaching plus email support is generous. I'd consider $9,500 or at least build in a rate increase after Year 1.

Tier 3 is where the real value is. $18k for a year-long engagement is very reasonable for what you're offering. Most districts would see that as a deal. Don't undersell yourself here.

One suggestion: add a Discovery Call step before Tier 1. Free 30-minute call. Builds trust and lets you qualify leads without committing your time to people who aren't serious.

Send me the full business plan when it's ready. Happy to review over the holidays.

Rebecca

emails.subject: Facilities Expansion Planning  -  Principals Briefing
emails.content:
Principals,

As you may have seen in recent press coverage, the board has approved the long-range facilities master plan. I want to give you a direct briefing on what this means for your buildings and the district as a whole.

Key points:

1. Three new elementary schools and one middle school campus in NE Denver and Green Valley Ranch, construction starting 2026.
2. $380 million in bond-funded construction over 5 years.
3. Karen Delgado (Director of Facilities) is leading the project. Her team will be reaching out to principals for input on design standards and community engagement.
4. Existing buildings: maintenance and upgrade budgets will increase 15% starting FY26.
5. Enrollment projections for the northeast corridor are driving the expansion timeline.

I will be scheduling individual briefings with principals in the affected zones over the next month. For those of you outside the expansion zone, this still matters  -  redistricting conversations will follow.

This is internal until the district releases the public summary in February. Keep details inside the principals circle until then.

Questions? Bring them to your next one-on-one.

Greg Holloway
Superintendent, Denver Public Schools

emails.subject: Professional Development Outlook  -  Due April 4
emails.content:
Principals,

As part of the 5-year leadership vision process, I am asking each principal to submit a brief professional development outlook alongside their vision plan.

Specifically, I would like to know:

1. What professional development are you currently pursuing or planning (certifications, graduate work, workshops)?
2. Are you involved in any outside consulting, advisory, or coaching work that informs your practice?
3. How do you see your professional growth aligning with your school’s trajectory over the next 3-5 years?

This is not a compliance exercise. I genuinely want to understand where your energy is going so we can support your growth and align it with district priorities. If you are exploring anything outside the district, even informally, I would rather know about it now than be surprised later.

Please submit by April 4. A short paragraph for each question is fine.

Greg Holloway
Superintendent, Denver Public Schools
